articles.headline: India Advocates for Inclusive Global AI Governance Framework at UN Summit
articles.explainer:
• India presented its vision for a multi-stakeholder and inclusive global AI governance framework at the UN General Assembly's High-Level Summit on Artificial Intelligence.
• Emphasized balancing innovation with ethical considerations, data privacy, and mitigating potential biases in AI systems.
• Proposed a "Delhi Declaration on Responsible AI" calling for international collaboration, capacity building, and equitable access to AI benefits for developing nations.
• Highlighted the need for a common regulatory language and interoperable standards to prevent digital divides and ensure AI's benefits are universally shared.
• Underlined India's commitment to using AI for social good, citing initiatives like AI-powered healthcare and agricultural solutions.
gsSubTopics: International institutions
India and its neighbourhood- relations
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights
prelimsPoints: UN General Assembly High-Level Summit on AI
India's proposal for global AI governance
Key pillars: ethics, privacy, bias mitigation, innovation
Concept of 'Delhi Declaration on Responsible AI'
mainsPoints: Discuss India's role in shaping global technology governance and its implications for developing nations.
Analyze the challenges and opportunities of AI for global development, emphasizing ethical considerations and equitable access.
Examine the need for a multi-stakeholder approach in formulating international norms for emerging technologies.
Evaluate how India's stance on AI governance aligns with its broader foreign policy objectives and digital public infrastructure strategy.
articles.imageDescription: Delegates at a UN summit discussing AI, with India's flag visible.
articles.trivia: India is one of the fastest-growing AI markets globally, with significant applications across various sectors.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds 'Right to be Forgotten' in Digital Space, Sets Guidelines
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark judgment, affirmed the 'right to be forgotten' as an integral part of the right to privacy under Article 21 of the Constitution.
• The ruling mandates search engines and online platforms to delist personal information upon request, provided certain criteria are met, balancing individual privacy with public interest and freedom of expression.
• Guidelines were issued for assessing requests, considering factors like the nature of information, its sensitivity, public role of the individual, and the impact on their reputation.
• This decision is expected to have significant implications for data protection laws and the digital rights of citizens in India.
• The court emphasized the need for a robust data protection framework to effectively implement this right.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Functioning of the Judiciary
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Right to Privacy
Data Protection
prelimsPoints: Supreme Court's ruling on 'right to be forgotten'
Linked to Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty)
Balance between privacy, free speech, and public interest
Implications for search engines and online platforms
mainsPoints: Analyze the constitutional basis and implications of the 'right to be forgotten' in India's digital age.
Discuss the challenges in implementing the 'right to be forgotten' while upholding freedom of expression and access to information.
Evaluate the role of the judiciary in evolving data protection norms in the absence of a comprehensive data protection law.
Examine how this ruling impacts individuals' control over their digital footprint and the responsibilities of online intermediaries.
articles.imageDescription: A gavel resting on a law book, symbolizing a judicial decision, with digital elements in the background.
articles.trivia: The 'right to be forgotten' originated in European Union law with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Indian Polity and Governance
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Right to Privacy and associated rights

articles.headline: India's Q2 GDP Growth Exceeds Expectations, Manufacturing Sector Leads Recovery
articles.explainer:
• The National Statistical Office (NSO) announced that India's Gross Domestic Product (GDP) grew by 7.8% in the second quarter (July-September) of FY 2025-26, surpassing market forecasts.
• The manufacturing sector emerged as a key driver, registering robust growth due to increased domestic demand and government-led production incentives.
• Services sector also maintained strong momentum, particularly in financial, real estate, and professional services.
• Agriculture sector showed steady growth, supported by a favorable monsoon and government support policies.
• Analysts attribute the strong performance to sustained capital expenditure by the government and a resurgence in private consumption.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting

Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth
prelimsPoints: India's Q2 FY25-26 GDP growth rate: 7.8%
Key contributing sectors: Manufacturing, Services, Agriculture
Role of NSO in releasing GDP data
Factors: Government capital expenditure, private consumption
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to India's robust Q2 GDP growth, particularly the role of the manufacturing sector.
Discuss the implications of this growth trajectory for employment generation and income distribution in India.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government policies, such as production incentives and capital expenditure, in stimulating economic recovery.
Examine the challenges that persist for sustained economic growth, including global headwinds and inflationary pressures.
articles.imageDescription: A graph showing an upward trend, symbolizing economic growth, with Indian currency notes.
articles.trivia: India's economy is projected to be among the fastest-growing major economies globally for several years.

articles.headline: Government Launches Comprehensive National Policy to Tackle Microplastic Pollution
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change has unveiled a new National Policy on Microplastic Pollution, aiming for a significant reduction in plastic waste discharge by 2030.
• The policy focuses on a multi-pronged approach including source reduction, improved waste collection and recycling infrastructure, and promoting research into biodegradable alternatives.
• It mandates extended producer responsibility (EPR) for manufacturers and importers of plastic products, covering the entire lifecycle of plastics.
• Public awareness campaigns and citizen participation are integral components, emphasizing behavioral change towards plastic consumption.
• Specific targets have been set for phasing out single-use plastics and investing in advanced technologies for plastic waste treatment.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
prelimsPoints: National Policy on Microplastic Pollution by MoEFCC
Target for plastic waste reduction: 2030
Key strategies: source reduction, recycling, biodegradable alternatives
Mandates Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)
Focus on phasing out single-use plastics
mainsPoints: Analyze the multifaceted challenges posed by microplastic pollution to India's environment and public health.
Critically evaluate the provisions of the new National Policy on Microplastic Pollution, including its strengths and potential shortcomings.
Discuss the role of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) and behavioral change in achieving sustainable plastic waste management.
Suggest additional measures and technological innovations required to effectively combat microplastic contamination in India.
articles.imageDescription: A hand holding a piece of plastic waste near a body of water, symbolizing pollution and conservation efforts.
articles.trivia: Microplastics are plastic particles smaller than 5 millimeters, often invisible to the naked eye, found globally.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Environment and Ecology
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Solid Waste Management and Plastic Pollution

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches Next-Gen Communication Satellite for Enhanced Broadband Services
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched the GSAT-24R communication satellite aboard the GSLV Mk-III rocket from Satish Dhawan Space Centre.
• GSAT-24R is a high-throughput satellite designed to provide advanced broadband connectivity, direct-to-home (DTH) services, and digital data transmission across India and its island territories.
• This mission marks a significant step towards bolstering India's indigenous satellite communication capabilities and reducing reliance on foreign transponders.
• The satellite features state-of-the-art Ku-band transponders, offering increased bandwidth and improved signal quality.
• The successful launch is expected to bridge the digital divide in remote areas and support initiatives like Digital India and BharatNet.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights
prelimsPoints: GSAT-24R: next-gen communication satellite
Launch vehicle: GSLV Mk-III
Purpose: broadband, DTH, digital data services
Features: Ku-band transponders
Launched by ISRO from Satish Dhawan Space Centre
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of indigenous communication satellites like GSAT-24R for India's digital infrastructure and national security.
Analyze how enhanced satellite broadband connectivity can contribute to bridging the digital divide and achieving goals of Digital India.
Evaluate ISRO's role in advancing India's self-reliance in space technology and its commercial potential.
Examine the challenges and opportunities in the global satellite communication market for Indian players.
articles.imageDescription: A GSLV Mk-III rocket lifting off into space with a trail of fire.
articles.trivia: ISRO holds the record for launching the most satellites in a single mission (104 satellites in 2017).

articles.headline: India Pledges Enhanced Support for Global South Climate Adaptation Fund at COP30 Preparatory Meet
articles.explainer:
• At a preparatory meeting for COP30, India reiterated its commitment to climate action and pledged significantly enhanced financial contributions to the Global South Climate Adaptation Fund.
• The fund, a multi-lateral initiative, aims to help developing nations build resilience against the impacts of climate change, focusing on infrastructure, early warning systems, and sustainable agriculture.
• India emphasized the principle of Common But Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC), urging developed nations to meet their climate finance commitments.
• The move underscores India's growing leadership role in advocating for climate justice and supporting fellow developing countries.
• India also highlighted its domestic achievements in renewable energy deployment and climate-resilient development.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
prelimsPoints: India's pledge at COP30 preparatory meeting
Global South Climate Adaptation Fund
Principle of Common But Differentiated Responsibilities (CBDR-RC)
Focus areas: infrastructure, early warning, sustainable agriculture
mainsPoints: Discuss India's evolving role in global climate diplomacy, particularly its advocacy for the Global South.
Analyze the significance of climate finance and adaptation funds for developing countries in combating climate change.
Evaluate the principle of CBDR-RC in the context of current climate negotiations and the responsibilities of developed nations.
Examine how India's domestic climate actions align with its international commitments and leadership aspirations.
articles.imageDescription: A world map with focus on developing nations, overlaid with symbols of renewable energy and climate protection.
articles.trivia: COP30 is scheduled to be held in Brazil in 2025.

articles.headline: Government Unveils Comprehensive National Cybersecurity Strategy 2.0 to Safeguard Digital Infrastructure
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) launched the National Cybersecurity Strategy 2.0, a significant upgrade to India's cyber defense framework.
• The new strategy emphasizes proactive threat intelligence, critical infrastructure protection, and enhanced public-private partnerships.
• It introduces a dedicated 'National Cyber Force' for rapid response to major cyber incidents and strengthens capacity building in cybersecurity skills.
• Focus areas include securing Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI), developing indigenous cybersecurity products, and fostering international collaboration against cyber threats.
• The strategy aims to create a resilient and secure cyberspace for citizens and businesses, aligning with India's vision for a digital economy.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
Cyber security
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life

Key features: Proactive threat intelligence, Critical Infrastructure Protection
Establishment of 'National Cyber Force'

Emphasis on indigenous product development and international collaboration
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolving landscape of cyber threats faced by India and the necessity of a comprehensive cybersecurity strategy.
Critically evaluate the key pillars of the National Cybersecurity Strategy 2.0, including its strengths and potential implementation challenges.
Discuss the role of public-private partnerships and international collaboration in strengthening India's cyber resilience.
Examine how the strategy contributes to safeguarding India's Digital Public Infrastructure and fostering trust in the digital economy.
articles.imageDescription: A padlock icon overlaid on a digital circuit board, symbolizing cybersecurity.
articles.trivia: India ranks among the top countries targeted by cyberattacks globally.

articles.headline: Major Expansion of 'PM Kaushal Vikas Yojana' to Boost Youth Employability Across Sectors
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship announced a significant expansion of the Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Vikas Yojana (PMKVY), targeting 50 million youth by 2030.
• The enhanced scheme introduces new industry-aligned courses in emerging technologies like AI, IoT, and green skills, in collaboration with industry leaders.
• A strong emphasis is placed on demand-driven skilling, with improved linkages to employment opportunities and entrepreneurial support.
• The expansion includes dedicated programs for women, persons with disabilities, and tribal communities, ensuring inclusive skill development.
• A new digital platform integrating skill assessment, certification, and job matching has been launched to streamline the process and enhance transparency.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: PMKVY expansion targets 50 million youth by 2030
New courses in emerging technologies (AI, IoT, green skills)
Focus on demand-driven skilling and employment linkages
Inclusion of women, PwD, tribal communities
New digital platform for skill assessment and job matching
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of skill development initiatives like PMKVY in addressing India's demographic dividend and unemployment challenges.
Critically evaluate the expanded PMKVY's potential to bridge the skill gap in emerging sectors and foster a future-ready workforce.
Discuss the importance of inclusive skill development for vulnerable sections of society and its contribution to equitable growth.
Examine the role of industry collaboration and digital platforms in enhancing the effectiveness and transparency of vocational training programs.
articles.imageDescription: Young people learning vocational skills in a modern training center, symbolizing skill development.
articles.trivia: PMKVY was launched in 2015 to provide skill training to Indian youth.

articles.headline: Parliament Passes Bill for Streamlining Tribunal Appointments and Functioning
articles.explainer:
• Both Houses of Parliament passed the Tribunals Reforms (Rationalisation and Conditions of Service) Bill, 2025, aimed at streamlining the appointment process and service conditions of members in various tribunals.
• The Bill seeks to enhance the efficiency and independence of tribunals by ensuring a more transparent and merit-based selection process.
• It proposes a centralized body for recommending appointments and standardizing the tenure and age limits across different tribunals.
• The reforms address long-standing concerns regarding vacancies, delays, and judicial oversight in tribunal functioning.
• Experts believe the legislation will reduce the burden on higher courts and improve access to justice for specialized disputes.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary Ministries and Departments of the Government; pressure groups and formal/informal associations and their role in the Polity
Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these
prelimsPoints: Tribunals Reforms (Rationalisation and Conditions of Service) Bill, 2025
Aims: streamline appointments, standardize service conditions
Proposes centralized appointment body
Objectives: reduce vacancies, improve efficiency, enhance independence
mainsPoints: Discuss the rationale behind the establishment of tribunals in India and the challenges they have faced in ensuring speedy justice.
Analyze the key provisions of the Tribunals Reforms Bill, 2025, and their potential impact on the autonomy and effectiveness of these bodies.
Evaluate how these reforms contribute to easing the burden on conventional courts and improving access to justice for specialized disputes.
Examine the constitutional concerns, if any, regarding the balance of powers between the executive and judiciary in tribunal appointments.
articles.imageDescription: The Parliament House of India, symbolizing legislative action.
articles.trivia: The concept of tribunals in India was introduced to reduce the workload of regular courts and provide specialized justice.

articles.headline: FDI Inflows Surge in Renewable Energy Sector, Signals Investor Confidence
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Commerce and Industry reported a significant surge in Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) inflows into India's renewable energy sector during the first half of FY 2025-26.
• The increase is attributed to India's ambitious renewable energy targets, supportive policy framework, and growing global emphasis on green investments.
• Major investments were observed in solar power projects, offshore wind energy, and green hydrogen production facilities.
• This trend indicates strong international investor confidence in India's clean energy transition and its potential as a global manufacturing hub for renewable technologies.
• The government highlighted the role of the Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme for solar PV modules and battery storage in attracting these investments.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Investment models
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
prelimsPoints: FDI surge in renewable energy sector
Key drivers: Ambitious targets, supportive policies, global green investment
Major investments in solar, offshore wind, green hydrogen
Role of PLI scheme for solar PV and battery storage
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to the surge in FDI in India's renewable energy sector and its significance for energy security.
Discuss the role of government policies, such as the PLI scheme and ambitious targets, in attracting green investments.
Evaluate the potential of India to become a global hub for renewable energy manufacturing and green hydrogen production.
Examine the challenges and opportunities in scaling up renewable energy infrastructure and integrating it into the national grid.
articles.imageDescription: Solar panels and wind turbines against a blue sky, symbolizing renewable energy.
articles.trivia: India aims to achieve 500 GW of non-fossil energy capacity by 2030.

articles.headline: Western Ghats Facing Increased Biodiversity Threat from Climate Change and Anthropogenic Pressures: New Report
articles.explainer:
• A comprehensive report by the Indian Institute of Science (IISc) and the Wildlife Institute of India (WII) warns of escalating biodiversity threats to the Western Ghats.
• The report highlights the combined impact of climate change-induced extreme weather events, habitat fragmentation, and unchecked urbanization.
• It identifies several endemic species, particularly amphibians and flowering plants, as highly vulnerable to extinction.
• Recommendations include strengthening protected area networks, implementing stricter environmental impact assessments, and promoting eco-friendly development practices.
• The study underscores the urgency of integrated conservation strategies to preserve this UNESCO World Heritage Site and biodiversity hotspot.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Biodiversity and its conservation
Environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
prelimsPoints: Report on Western Ghats biodiversity by IISc and WII
Key threats: climate change, habitat fragmentation, urbanization
Vulnerable species: amphibians, flowering plants
Recommendations: stronger protected areas, stricter EIAs
Western Ghats: UNESCO World Heritage Site, biodiversity hotspot
mainsPoints: Discuss the ecological significance of the Western Ghats as a biodiversity hotspot and the various threats it faces.
Analyze the interconnectedness of climate change, anthropogenic pressures, and biodiversity loss in the region.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current conservation strategies and suggest comprehensive measures for protecting the Western Ghats.
Examine the challenges in balancing development aspirations with environmental conservation in ecologically sensitive zones.
articles.imageDescription: Lush green landscape of the Western Ghats with a waterfall, symbolizing its biodiversity.
articles.trivia: The Western Ghats is older than the Himalayas and is home to at least 325 globally threatened species.

articles.headline: India-ASEAN Maritime Security Dialogue Concludes with Joint Declaration on Regional Cooperation
articles.explainer:
• The annual India-ASEAN Maritime Security Dialogue concluded in New Delhi with a joint declaration reaffirming commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific.
• Discussions focused on enhancing cooperation in maritime domain awareness, combating piracy, search and rescue operations, and addressing challenges to freedom of navigation.
• Both sides emphasized the importance of adherence to international law, particularly the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), for peaceful resolution of disputes.
• The dialogue also explored avenues for joint naval exercises, capacity building for ASEAN member states, and information sharing mechanisms.
• This engagement underscores India's 'Act East' policy and its strategic partnership with ASEAN in maintaining regional stability.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India-ASEAN Maritime Security Dialogue location: New Delhi
Joint declaration on free, open, inclusive Indo-Pacific
Focus areas: MDA, anti-piracy, search & rescue, freedom of navigation
Importance of UNCLOS emphasized
Dialogue aligns with India's 'Act East' policy
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic significance of India's maritime cooperation with ASEAN in the context of its 'Act East' policy and Indo-Pacific vision.
Discuss the common maritime security challenges faced by India and ASEAN nations and the importance of a coordinated regional response.
Evaluate the role of international law, particularly UNCLOS, in ensuring peace and stability in the Indo-Pacific maritime domain.
Examine the potential for enhanced naval cooperation, capacity building, and information sharing between India and ASEAN member states.
articles.imageDescription: Naval ships sailing in formation, symbolizing maritime cooperation and security.
articles.trivia: ASEAN (Association of Southeast Asian Nations) was founded in 1967.

articles.headline: Indian Researchers Announce Breakthrough in Affordable Diagnostics for Non-Communicable Diseases
articles.explainer:
• A team of scientists from the Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Delhi and AIIMS has developed a cost-effective and rapid diagnostic platform for early detection of several Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs).
• The innovative technology utilizes AI-powered analysis of biomarkers, offering results within minutes with high accuracy, reducing the need for expensive lab infrastructure.
• This breakthrough is particularly significant for rural and underserved populations, where access to advanced diagnostic facilities for NCDs like diabetes, cardiovascular diseases, and certain cancers is limited.
• The platform is designed to be portable and user-friendly, enabling point-of-care testing and timely intervention.
• The initiative aligns with the National Health Mission's goal of strengthening primary healthcare and addressing the growing burden of NCDs in India.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: Breakthrough in NCD diagnostics by IIT Delhi and AIIMS
Features: Cost-effective, rapid, AI-powered biomarker analysis
Significance for rural and underserved areas
Targets NCDs like diabetes, cardiovascular diseases, cancers
Aligns with National Health Mission goals
mainsPoints: Discuss the increasing burden of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) in India and the challenges in their early detection and management.
Analyze how indigenous innovations in medical diagnostics, like the one developed by IIT Delhi and AIIMS, can revolutionize healthcare access and affordability.
Evaluate the potential of AI and portable diagnostic technologies to strengthen primary healthcare and reduce health disparities in India.
Examine the steps required to facilitate the widespread adoption and scaling of such technological breakthroughs across the public health system.
articles.imageDescription: A medical professional operating a portable diagnostic device, symbolizing advanced healthcare.
articles.trivia: NCDs account for over 60% of all deaths in India.

articles.headline: Central Government Forms High-Level Panel to Review and Enhance E-Governance Initiatives
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Personnel, Public Grievances and Pensions has constituted a high-level expert committee to review the progress and effectiveness of various e-governance initiatives across India.
• The panel is tasked with identifying bottlenecks, suggesting technological upgrades, and recommending measures to enhance citizen-centric service delivery.
• Focus areas include improving interoperability between government departments, strengthening data security, and ensuring last-mile digital inclusion.
• The committee will also assess the impact of existing e-governance projects on transparency, accountability, and efficiency of public administration.
• Recommendations are expected to shape the next phase of India's digital transformation strategy, aiming for seamless and equitable access to government services.
gsSubTopics: Governance, Transparency and Accountability, E-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential; citizens charters, transparency & accountability and institutional and other measures.
Role of civil services in a democracy
prelimsPoints: High-level panel formed by Ministry of Personnel, Public Grievances and Pensions
Purpose: Review and enhance e-governance initiatives
Focus: Interoperability, data security, digital inclusion
Aim: Improve transparency, accountability, efficiency in public administration
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolution of e-governance in India and its role in improving public service delivery and reducing corruption.
Analyze the key challenges faced by e-governance initiatives, such as digital divide, data security, and interoperability issues.
Evaluate the potential impact of the expert committee's recommendations on enhancing citizen-centric governance and digital inclusion.
Examine how e-governance can be further leveraged to strengthen transparency, accountability, and participatory democracy in India.
articles.imageDescription: A hand interacting with a digital interface showing government services, symbolizing e-governance.
articles.trivia: The National e-Governance Plan (NeGP) was launched in 2006 to lay the foundation for e-governance in India.

articles.headline: New Agricultural Export Policy Aims for Doubling Farmers' Income by Boosting Value-Added Exports
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Commerce and Industry, in collaboration with the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare, launched a new Agricultural Export Policy.
• The policy's primary objective is to double farmers' income by promoting value-added agricultural exports and reducing post-harvest losses.
• Key strategies include developing export-oriented agricultural clusters, investing in agri-infrastructure like cold chains and processing units, and simplifying export procedures.
• It also focuses on promoting organic and GI-tagged products, enhancing market access, and building brand India in global agricultural markets.
• The policy aims to diversify India's export basket and ensure stable and remunerative prices for farmers.
gsSubTopics: Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers
Economics of animal-rearing
Food processing and related industries in India- scope’ and significance, location, upstream and downstream requirements, supply chain management
Public Distribution System—objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
prelimsPoints: New Agricultural Export Policy by Ministries of Commerce & Agriculture
Objective: Double farmers' income
Focus: Value-added exports, reduced post-harvest losses
Strategies: Agri-clusters, cold chains, simplified procedures
Promotion of organic and GI-tagged products
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the new Agricultural Export Policy in achieving the goal of doubling farmers' income and enhancing India's agricultural competitiveness.
Discuss the role of value addition, infrastructure development, and market access in transforming India's agricultural export landscape.
Evaluate the potential of promoting organic and GI-tagged products in global markets for remunerative returns to farmers.
Examine the challenges in implementing the policy, including supply chain inefficiencies, quality standards, and global trade barriers.
articles.imageDescription: Fresh agricultural produce being loaded onto a container ship, symbolizing agricultural exports.
articles.trivia: India is one of the world's largest producers of several agricultural commodities.

articles.headline: National Literacy Mission Targets Remote Tribal Areas in New Phase of 'Sakshar Bharat' Program
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education announced the launch of a new phase of the 'Sakshar Bharat' program under the National Literacy Mission, specifically targeting remote tribal areas.
• The initiative aims to achieve functional literacy among adult illiterates, particularly women and marginalized groups, through community-led learning centers.
• Special emphasis will be placed on developing culturally sensitive learning materials and employing local instructors to foster greater participation.
• The program integrates digital literacy and financial literacy modules to empower beneficiaries with essential skills for modern life.
• This renewed focus seeks to address the persistent literacy gaps in hard-to-reach regions and promote inclusive educational development.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India
Role of women and women’s organization, population and associated issues, poverty and developmental issues, urbanization, their problems and their remedies
Effects of globalization on Indian society
Social empowerment, communalism, regionalism & secularism
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources

Target: Remote tribal areas, adult illiterates (women, marginalized)
Strategies: Community-led centers, culturally sensitive materials, local instructors

mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of adult literacy programs in achieving comprehensive human development and social empowerment in India.
Analyze the specific challenges faced in promoting literacy among tribal communities and the need for culturally sensitive approaches.
Evaluate how the integration of digital and financial literacy can empower marginalized groups and bridge the development gap.
Examine the role of community participation and local leadership in the successful implementation of literacy initiatives in remote areas.
articles.imageDescription: Adult learners, including tribal individuals, participating in a literacy class in a rural setting.
articles.trivia: The National Literacy Mission was launched in 1988 with the aim of imparting functional literacy to non-literates in the 15-35 age group.

articles.headline: Border Roads Organisation Completes Strategic Tunnel in Eastern Himalayas, Enhancing All-Weather Connectivity
articles.explainer:
• The Border Roads Organisation (BRO) announced the completion of a crucial strategic tunnel in the Eastern Himalayas, significantly boosting all-weather connectivity to forward areas.
• The tunnel, constructed at an altitude of over 12,000 feet, reduces travel time by several hours and bypasses treacherous avalanche-prone zones.
• This infrastructure development is vital for military logistics, facilitating rapid troop and equipment movement to the Line of Actual Control (LAC).
• It also promises to enhance socio-economic development in remote border regions by improving access for local communities and promoting tourism.
• The project underscores India's commitment to strengthening its border infrastructure and bolstering national security in challenging terrains.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Role of external state and non-state actors in creating challenges to internal security
Salient features of world’s physical geography

Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
prelimsPoints: Strategic tunnel completed by BRO in Eastern Himalayas
Altitude: over 12,000 feet
Benefits: reduced travel time, bypasses avalanche zones, military logistics
Enhances connectivity to Line of Actual Control (LAC)
Contributes to socio-economic development of border regions
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of border infrastructure development, particularly in the Eastern Himalayas, for India's national security.

Examine the geopolitical implications of enhanced connectivity along the Line of Actual Control (LAC) and its impact on regional stability.
articles.imageDescription: The entrance of a newly constructed tunnel in a mountainous, snowy region.
articles.trivia: The BRO is responsible for building and maintaining road networks in India's border areas and friendly neighboring countries.

articles.headline: Indian Army Conducts Integrated Tri-Service Exercise 'Sagar Prahar' in Eastern Sector
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Army, in coordination with the Indian Navy and Indian Air Force, successfully conducted a large-scale integrated tri-service exercise named 'Sagar Prahar' in the Eastern Sector.
• The exercise focused on enhancing jointness, interoperability, and rapid response capabilities across all three services in a complex, multi-domain operational scenario.
• Key drills included amphibious landings, air-sea coordinated attacks, special forces operations, and logistics support in challenging coastal and island territories.
• The exercise aimed to validate new doctrines and technologies for integrated theatre commands and strengthen India's defense posture in the region.
• 'Sagar Prahar' simulated responses to potential threats, reinforcing India's commitment to safeguarding its maritime interests and territorial integrity.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
Role of external state and non-state actors in creating challenges to internal security
prelimsPoints: Tri-service exercise 'Sagar Prahar' conducted in Eastern Sector
Involved Indian Army, Navy, Air Force
Focus: jointness, interoperability, rapid response
Included: amphibious landings, air-sea attacks, special forces ops
Aimed at validating integrated theatre command doctrines
mainsPoints: Discuss the importance of integrated tri-service exercises in enhancing India's defense preparedness and strategic capabilities.
Analyze the concept of theatre commands and how such exercises contribute to their operationalization and effectiveness.
Evaluate the challenges and benefits of achieving seamless jointness and interoperability among the three armed services.
Examine the significance of 'Sagar Prahar' in strengthening India's defense posture in the Eastern Sector, considering regional geopolitical dynamics.
articles.imageDescription: Military personnel from Army, Navy, and Air Force participating in a joint exercise.
articles.trivia: The Indian Armed Forces are the second-largest military force in the world.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Internal Security
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Indian Armed Forces and their capabilities

articles.headline: NITI Aayog Report Highlights Need for Integrated Urban Planning for Sustainable Urbanization
articles.explainer:
• NITI Aayog released a comprehensive report titled 'Reimagining India's Urban Future: Towards Sustainable and Inclusive Cities', advocating for integrated urban planning.
• The report emphasizes the urgency of addressing challenges like affordable housing, public transport, waste management, and climate resilience in rapidly urbanizing areas.
• It recommends a multi-level governance approach, empowering local bodies with greater financial and administrative autonomy for urban development.
• The report also pushes for technology adoption, data-driven planning, and public-private partnerships to build smart, sustainable cities.
• This publication is expected to guide policy formulation for India's urban growth, aligning with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).
gsSubTopics: Urbanization, their problems and their remedies
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India
Effects of globalization on Indian society
Population and associated issues, poverty and developmental issues, urbanization, their problems and their remedies

Focus: Integrated urban planning, sustainable cities
Key challenges: housing, transport, waste, climate resilience
Recommendations: multi-level governance, technology adoption, PPPs
Aligns with Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)
mainsPoints: Analyze the key challenges and opportunities associated with rapid urbanization in India and their implications for sustainable development.
Discuss the recommendations of the NITI Aayog report for integrated urban planning, including multi-level governance and technology adoption.
Evaluate the role of smart city initiatives and public-private partnerships in building climate-resilient and inclusive urban spaces.
Examine how policy reforms in urban governance can contribute to achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) in India's cities.
articles.imageDescription: A futuristic city skyline with green spaces, symbolizing sustainable urban development.
articles.trivia: By 2050, it is estimated that over 50% of India's population will live in urban areas.

highlights: India proposes global AI governance framework at UN Summit, advocating for ethical AI and equitable access for developing nations.
Supreme Court upholds 'Right to be Forgotten' as part of privacy, setting guidelines for delisting personal digital information.
India's Q2 GDP growth surges to 7.8%, driven by robust manufacturing and services sectors, exceeding market expectations.
Government launches comprehensive National Policy to tackle microplastic pollution, mandating EPR and targeting reduction by 2030.
ISRO successfully launches next-gen communication satellite GSAT-24R, enhancing broadband and DTH services across India.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the 'Right to be Forgotten' recently upheld by the Supreme Court, consider the following statements:
1. It is explicitly mentioned as a fundamental right under Article 19 of the Indian Constitution.
2. The Supreme Court ruling mandates online platforms to delist personal information only if it is proven to be false or defamatory.
3. The ruling balances individual privacy with public interest and freedom of expression.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Supreme Court has affirmed the 'right to be forgotten' as an integral part of the right to privacy under Article 21, not Article 19. The ruling mandates delisting personal information upon request, provided certain criteria are met, balancing individual privacy with public interest and freedom of expression, not solely based on falsity or defamation.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following statements best describes the primary objective of the new National Policy on Microplastic Pollution?
options: (a) To ban all forms of plastic production and import in India by 2028.
(b) To promote the export of recycled plastic products to boost the circular economy.
(c) To achieve a significant reduction in plastic waste discharge by 2030 through source reduction, improved recycling, and biodegradable alternatives.
(d) To establish international collaborations for deep-sea microplastic clean-up operations.
prelimsMocks.answer: (c) To achieve a significant reduction in plastic waste discharge by 2030 through source reduction, improved recycling, and biodegradable alternatives.
prelimsMocks.explanation: The National Policy on Microplastic Pollution aims for a significant reduction in plastic waste discharge by 2030, focusing on source reduction, improved waste collection and recycling, and promoting research into biodegradable alternatives. It does not aim to ban all plastic production or solely focus on export or deep-sea clean-up.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following about the recently launched GSAT-24R satellite:
1. It was launched using the PSLV-C51 rocket.
2. It is a communication satellite designed for high-throughput broadband services.
3. It primarily uses C-band transponders for its operations.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: The GSAT-24R communication satellite was launched aboard the GSLV Mk-III rocket, not PSLV-C51. It is a high-throughput communication satellite providing advanced broadband connectivity and DTH services. It features state-of-the-art Ku-band transponders, not C-band.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the Global South Climate Adaptation Fund, which India pledged enhanced support for, consider the following statements:
1. Its primary aim is to facilitate technology transfer for renewable energy projects in developed nations.
2. India emphasized the principle of Common But Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC) in its support.
3. The fund focuses on helping developing nations build resilience against climate change impacts.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Global South Climate Adaptation Fund aims to help developing nations build resilience against climate change impacts, not to facilitate technology transfer for renewable energy projects in developed nations. India did emphasize the principle of CBDR-RC.

prelimsMocks.question: The 'Sagar Prahar' exercise, recently conducted by the Indian Armed Forces, focused on:
options: (a) Joint humanitarian aid and disaster relief operations in the Indian Ocean region.
(b) Enhancing interoperability and rapid response capabilities across all three services in the Eastern Sector.
(c) Testing new indigenous ballistic missile defense systems in the Arabian Sea.
(d) Bilateral counter-terrorism drills with a friendly neighboring country.
prelimsMocks.answer: (b) Enhancing interoperability and rapid response capabilities across all three services in the Eastern Sector.
prelimsMocks.explanation: The 'Sagar Prahar' exercise was a large-scale integrated tri-service exercise conducted in the Eastern Sector, focusing on enhancing jointness, interoperability, and rapid response capabilities across the Indian Army, Navy, and Air Force in a complex, multi-domain operational scenario.

mainsMocks.question: India's recent advocacy for an inclusive global AI governance framework at the UN Summit reflects its growing influence in shaping international technological norms. Discuss the ethical, economic, and geopolitical implications of AI, and analyze India's potential role in ensuring equitable and responsible AI development globally. (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate; Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests; GS3: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce India's stance on AI governance. Discuss ethical implications (bias, privacy, accountability), economic (job displacement, growth, digital divide), and geopolitical (AI arms race, power dynamics). Analyze India's role: promoting multi-stakeholder approach, CBDR-RC for AI, leveraging DPI experience, bridging digital divide for Global South, and advocating for 'AI for All' approach.

mainsMocks.question: The Supreme Court's affirmation of the 'Right to be Forgotten' marks a significant step towards strengthening digital privacy in India. Critically examine the balance between an individual's right to control their personal information in the digital space and the principles of freedom of expression and public interest. What challenges might arise in its implementation, and how can they be addressed? (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure; Functioning of the Judiciary; Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
mainsMocks.approach: Define 'Right to be Forgotten' and its constitutional basis (Article 21). Discuss the delicate balance with Article 19(1)(a) (freedom of expression) and public interest (historical record, journalistic freedom). Outline implementation challenges: defining 'public interest', technical feasibility for platforms, cross-border data flows, potential for censorship. Suggest solutions: clear guidelines, independent oversight, robust data protection law, international cooperation.

mainsMocks.question: India's National Cybersecurity Strategy 2.0 aims to create a resilient and secure cyberspace. In light of evolving global cyber threats, discuss the key features and strategic imperatives of this updated policy. How crucial are public-private partnerships and indigenous capabilities in strengthening India's cyber resilience? (10 marks, 150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism; Cyber security; Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
mainsMocks.approach: Briefly describe the escalating cyber threat landscape. Outline key features of NCS 2.0: proactive threat intelligence, critical infrastructure protection, National Cyber Force, securing DPI, international collaboration. Explain the imperative of public-private partnerships for resource sharing, expertise, threat intelligence. Emphasize indigenous capabilities for self-reliance and customized solutions against state-sponsored threats and data localization.

quickBytes.text: The Satish Dhawan Space Centre (SDSC) SHAR, Sriharikota, is India's primary spaceport.
Article 21 of the Indian Constitution guarantees the Right to Life and Personal Liberty.
The National Statistical Office (NSO) is responsible for releasing macroeconomic data like GDP.
Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) makes manufacturers responsible for the post-consumer waste management of their products.
The UN Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS) defines rights and responsibilities of nations regarding their use of the world's oceans.
NITI Aayog, formed in 2015, acts as a think tank of the Government of India, providing policy inputs.
The Western Ghats is one of the world's eight 'hottest hotspots' of biological diversity.
PM Kaushal Vikas Yojana (PMKVY) is the flagship scheme of the Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship.
The Border Roads Organisation (BRO) constructs and maintains road networks in India’s border areas.
Geographical Indication (GI) tags are used on products that have a specific geographical origin and possess qualities or a reputation that are due to that origin.

GS1.headline: National Literacy Mission Targets Remote Tribal Areas in New Phase of 'Sakshar Bharat' Program
GS1.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education announced the launch of a new phase of the 'Sakshar Bharat' program under the National Literacy Mission, specifically targeting remote tribal areas.
• The initiative aims to achieve functional literacy among adult illiterates, particularly women and marginalized groups, through community-led learning centers.
• Special emphasis will be placed on developing culturally sensitive learning materials and employing local instructors to foster greater participation.
• The program integrates digital literacy and financial literacy modules to empower beneficiaries with essential skills for modern life.
• This renewed focus seeks to address the persistent literacy gaps in hard-to-reach regions and promote inclusive educational development.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India
Role of women and women’s organization, population and associated issues, poverty and developmental issues, urbanization, their problems and their remedies
Effects of globalization on Indian society
Social empowerment, communalism, regionalism & secularism
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: New phase of 'Sakshar Bharat' under National Literacy Mission
Target: Remote tribal areas, adult illiterates (women, marginalized)
Strategies: Community-led centers, culturally sensitive materials, local instructors
Includes digital and financial literacy modules
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of adult literacy programs in achieving comprehensive human development and social empowerment in India.
Analyze the specific challenges faced in promoting literacy among tribal communities and the need for culturally sensitive approaches.
Evaluate how the integration of digital and financial literacy can empower marginalized groups and bridge the development gap.
Examine the role of community participation and local leadership in the successful implementation of literacy initiatives in remote areas.
GS1.imageDescription: Adult learners, including tribal individuals, participating in a literacy class in a rural setting.
GS1.trivia: The National Literacy Mission was launched in 1988 with the aim of imparting functional literacy to non-literates in the 15-35 age group.

• The report also pushes for technology adoption, data-driven planning, and public-private partnerships to build smart, sustainable cities.

GS2.headline: India Advocates for Inclusive Global AI Governance Framework at UN Summit
GS2.explainer:
• India presented its vision for a multi-stakeholder and inclusive global AI governance framework at the UN General Assembly's High-Level Summit on Artificial Intelligence.
• Emphasized balancing innovation with ethical considerations, data privacy, and mitigating potential biases in AI systems.
• Proposed a "Delhi Declaration on Responsible AI" calling for international collaboration, capacity building, and equitable access to AI benefits for developing nations.
• Highlighted the need for a common regulatory language and interoperable standards to prevent digital divides and ensure AI's benefits are universally shared.
• Underlined India's commitment to using AI for social good, citing initiatives like AI-powered healthcare and agricultural solutions.
gsSubTopics: International institutions
India and its neighbourhood- relations
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights
prelimsPoints: UN General Assembly High-Level Summit on AI
India's proposal for global AI governance
Key pillars: ethics, privacy, bias mitigation, innovation
Concept of 'Delhi Declaration on Responsible AI'
mainsPoints: Discuss India's role in shaping global technology governance and its implications for developing nations.
Analyze the challenges and opportunities of AI for global development, emphasizing ethical considerations and equitable access.
Examine the need for a multi-stakeholder approach in formulating international norms for emerging technologies.
Evaluate how India's stance on AI governance aligns with its broader foreign policy objectives and digital public infrastructure strategy.
GS2.imageDescription: Delegates at a UN summit discussing AI, with India's flag visible.
GS2.trivia: India is one of the fastest-growing AI markets globally, with significant applications across various sectors.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Upholds 'Right to be Forgotten' in Digital Space, Sets Guidelines
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark judgment, affirmed the 'right to be forgotten' as an integral part of the right to privacy under Article 21 of the Constitution.
• The ruling mandates search engines and online platforms to delist personal information upon request, provided certain criteria are met, balancing individual privacy with public interest and freedom of expression.
• Guidelines were issued for assessing requests, considering factors like the nature of information, its sensitivity, public role of the individual, and the impact on their reputation.
• This decision is expected to have significant implications for data protection laws and the digital rights of citizens in India.
• The court emphasized the need for a robust data protection framework to effectively implement this right.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Functioning of the Judiciary
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Right to Privacy
Data Protection
prelimsPoints: Supreme Court's ruling on 'right to be forgotten'
Linked to Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty)
Balance between privacy, free speech, and public interest
Implications for search engines and online platforms
mainsPoints: Analyze the constitutional basis and implications of the 'right to be forgotten' in India's digital age.
Discuss the challenges in implementing the 'right to be forgotten' while upholding freedom of expression and access to information.
Evaluate the role of the judiciary in evolving data protection norms in the absence of a comprehensive data protection law.
Examine how this ruling impacts individuals' control over their digital footprint and the responsibilities of online intermediaries.
GS2.imageDescription: A gavel resting on a law book, symbolizing a judicial decision, with digital elements in the background.
GS2.trivia: The 'right to be forgotten' originated in European Union law with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Indian Polity and Governance
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Right to Privacy and associated rights

GS2.headline: India Pledges Enhanced Support for Global South Climate Adaptation Fund at COP30 Preparatory Meet
GS2.explainer:
• At a preparatory meeting for COP30, India reiterated its commitment to climate action and pledged significantly enhanced financial contributions to the Global South Climate Adaptation Fund.
• The fund, a multi-lateral initiative, aims to help developing nations build resilience against the impacts of climate change, focusing on infrastructure, early warning systems, and sustainable agriculture.
• India emphasized the principle of Common But Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC), urging developed nations to meet their climate finance commitments.
• The move underscores India's growing leadership role in advocating for climate justice and supporting fellow developing countries.
• India also highlighted its domestic achievements in renewable energy deployment and climate-resilient development.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
prelimsPoints: India's pledge at COP30 preparatory meeting
Global South Climate Adaptation Fund
Principle of Common But Differentiated Responsibilities (CBDR-RC)
Focus areas: infrastructure, early warning, sustainable agriculture
mainsPoints: Discuss India's evolving role in global climate diplomacy, particularly its advocacy for the Global South.
Analyze the significance of climate finance and adaptation funds for developing countries in combating climate change.
Evaluate the principle of CBDR-RC in the context of current climate negotiations and the responsibilities of developed nations.
Examine how India's domestic climate actions align with its international commitments and leadership aspirations.
GS2.imageDescription: A world map with focus on developing nations, overlaid with symbols of renewable energy and climate protection.
GS2.trivia: COP30 is scheduled to be held in Brazil in 2025.

GS2.headline: Major Expansion of 'PM Kaushal Vikas Yojana' to Boost Youth Employability Across Sectors
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship announced a significant expansion of the Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Vikas Yojana (PMKVY), targeting 50 million youth by 2030.
• The enhanced scheme introduces new industry-aligned courses in emerging technologies like AI, IoT, and green skills, in collaboration with industry leaders.
• A strong emphasis is placed on demand-driven skilling, with improved linkages to employment opportunities and entrepreneurial support.
• The expansion includes dedicated programs for women, persons with disabilities, and tribal communities, ensuring inclusive skill development.
• A new digital platform integrating skill assessment, certification, and job matching has been launched to streamline the process and enhance transparency.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: PMKVY expansion targets 50 million youth by 2030
New courses in emerging technologies (AI, IoT, green skills)
Focus on demand-driven skilling and employment linkages
Inclusion of women, PwD, tribal communities
New digital platform for skill assessment and job matching
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of skill development initiatives like PMKVY in addressing India's demographic dividend and unemployment challenges.
Critically evaluate the expanded PMKVY's potential to bridge the skill gap in emerging sectors and foster a future-ready workforce.
Discuss the importance of inclusive skill development for vulnerable sections of society and its contribution to equitable growth.
Examine the role of industry collaboration and digital platforms in enhancing the effectiveness and transparency of vocational training programs.
GS2.imageDescription: Young people learning vocational skills in a modern training center, symbolizing skill development.
GS2.trivia: PMKVY was launched in 2015 to provide skill training to Indian youth.

GS2.headline: Parliament Passes Bill for Streamlining Tribunal Appointments and Functioning
GS2.explainer:
• Both Houses of Parliament passed the Tribunals Reforms (Rationalisation and Conditions of Service) Bill, 2025, aimed at streamlining the appointment process and service conditions of members in various tribunals.
• The Bill seeks to enhance the efficiency and independence of tribunals by ensuring a more transparent and merit-based selection process.
• It proposes a centralized body for recommending appointments and standardizing the tenure and age limits across different tribunals.
• The reforms address long-standing concerns regarding vacancies, delays, and judicial oversight in tribunal functioning.
• Experts believe the legislation will reduce the burden on higher courts and improve access to justice for specialized disputes.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary Ministries and Departments of the Government; pressure groups and formal/informal associations and their role in the Polity
Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these
prelimsPoints: Tribunals Reforms (Rationalisation and Conditions of Service) Bill, 2025
Aims: streamline appointments, standardize service conditions
Proposes centralized appointment body
Objectives: reduce vacancies, improve efficiency, enhance independence
mainsPoints: Discuss the rationale behind the establishment of tribunals in India and the challenges they have faced in ensuring speedy justice.
Analyze the key provisions of the Tribunals Reforms Bill, 2025, and their potential impact on the autonomy and effectiveness of these bodies.
Evaluate how these reforms contribute to easing the burden on conventional courts and improving access to justice for specialized disputes.
Examine the constitutional concerns, if any, regarding the balance of powers between the executive and judiciary in tribunal appointments.
GS2.imageDescription: The Parliament House of India, symbolizing legislative action.
GS2.trivia: The concept of tribunals in India was introduced to reduce the workload of regular courts and provide specialized justice.

GS2.headline: India-ASEAN Maritime Security Dialogue Concludes with Joint Declaration on Regional Cooperation
GS2.explainer:
• The annual India-ASEAN Maritime Security Dialogue concluded in New Delhi with a joint declaration reaffirming commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific.
• Discussions focused on enhancing cooperation in maritime domain awareness, combating piracy, search and rescue operations, and addressing challenges to freedom of navigation.
• Both sides emphasized the importance of adherence to international law, particularly the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), for peaceful resolution of disputes.
• The dialogue also explored avenues for joint naval exercises, capacity building for ASEAN member states, and information sharing mechanisms.
• This engagement underscores India's 'Act East' policy and its strategic partnership with ASEAN in maintaining regional stability.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests

Joint declaration on free, open, inclusive Indo-Pacific
Focus areas: MDA, anti-piracy, search & rescue, freedom of navigation
Importance of UNCLOS emphasized
Dialogue aligns with India's 'Act East' policy
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic significance of India's maritime cooperation with ASEAN in the context of its 'Act East' policy and Indo-Pacific vision.
Discuss the common maritime security challenges faced by India and ASEAN nations and the importance of a coordinated regional response.
Evaluate the role of international law, particularly UNCLOS, in ensuring peace and stability in the Indo-Pacific maritime domain.
Examine the potential for enhanced naval cooperation, capacity building, and information sharing between India and ASEAN member states.
GS2.imageDescription: Naval ships sailing in formation, symbolizing maritime cooperation and security.
GS2.trivia: ASEAN (Association of Southeast Asian Nations) was founded in 1967.

GS2.headline: Central Government Forms High-Level Panel to Review and Enhance E-Governance Initiatives
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Personnel, Public Grievances and Pensions has constituted a high-level expert committee to review the progress and effectiveness of various e-governance initiatives across India.
• The panel is tasked with identifying bottlenecks, suggesting technological upgrades, and recommending measures to enhance citizen-centric service delivery.
• Focus areas include improving interoperability between government departments, strengthening data security, and ensuring last-mile digital inclusion.
• The committee will also assess the impact of existing e-governance projects on transparency, accountability, and efficiency of public administration.
• Recommendations are expected to shape the next phase of India's digital transformation strategy, aiming for seamless and equitable access to government services.
gsSubTopics: Governance, Transparency and Accountability, E-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential; citizens charters, transparency & accountability and institutional and other measures.
Role of civil services in a democracy

mainsPoints: Discuss the evolution of e-governance in India and its role in improving public service delivery and reducing corruption.
Analyze the key challenges faced by e-governance initiatives, such as digital divide, data security, and interoperability issues.
Evaluate the potential impact of the expert committee's recommendations on enhancing citizen-centric governance and digital inclusion.
Examine how e-governance can be further leveraged to strengthen transparency, accountability, and participatory democracy in India.
GS2.imageDescription: A hand interacting with a digital interface showing government services, symbolizing e-governance.
GS2.trivia: The National e-Governance Plan (NeGP) was launched in 2006 to lay the foundation for e-governance in India.

GS3.headline: India's Q2 GDP Growth Exceeds Expectations, Manufacturing Sector Leads Recovery
GS3.explainer:
• The National Statistical Office (NSO) announced that India's Gross Domestic Product (GDP) grew by 7.8% in the second quarter (July-September) of FY 2025-26, surpassing market forecasts.
• The manufacturing sector emerged as a key driver, registering robust growth due to increased domestic demand and government-led production incentives.
• Services sector also maintained strong momentum, particularly in financial, real estate, and professional services.
• Agriculture sector showed steady growth, supported by a favorable monsoon and government support policies.
• Analysts attribute the strong performance to sustained capital expenditure by the government and a resurgence in private consumption.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting

Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth
prelimsPoints: India's Q2 FY25-26 GDP growth rate: 7.8%
Key contributing sectors: Manufacturing, Services, Agriculture
Role of NSO in releasing GDP data

Discuss the implications of this growth trajectory for employment generation and income distribution in India.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government policies, such as production incentives and capital expenditure, in stimulating economic recovery.
Examine the challenges that persist for sustained economic growth, including global headwinds and inflationary pressures.
GS3.imageDescription: A graph showing an upward trend, symbolizing economic growth, with Indian currency notes.
GS3.trivia: India's economy is projected to be among the fastest-growing major economies globally for several years.

GS3.headline: Government Launches Comprehensive National Policy to Tackle Microplastic Pollution
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change has unveiled a new National Policy on Microplastic Pollution, aiming for a significant reduction in plastic waste discharge by 2030.
• The policy focuses on a multi-pronged approach including source reduction, improved waste collection and recycling infrastructure, and promoting research into biodegradable alternatives.
• It mandates extended producer responsibility (EPR) for manufacturers and importers of plastic products, covering the entire lifecycle of plastics.
• Public awareness campaigns and citizen participation are integral components, emphasizing behavioral change towards plastic consumption.
• Specific targets have been set for phasing out single-use plastics and investing in advanced technologies for plastic waste treatment.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
prelimsPoints: National Policy on Microplastic Pollution by MoEFCC
Target for plastic waste reduction: 2030
Key strategies: source reduction, recycling, biodegradable alternatives
Mandates Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)
Focus on phasing out single-use plastics
mainsPoints: Analyze the multifaceted challenges posed by microplastic pollution to India's environment and public health.
Critically evaluate the provisions of the new National Policy on Microplastic Pollution, including its strengths and potential shortcomings.
Discuss the role of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) and behavioral change in achieving sustainable plastic waste management.
Suggest additional measures and technological innovations required to effectively combat microplastic contamination in India.
GS3.imageDescription: A hand holding a piece of plastic waste near a body of water, symbolizing pollution and conservation efforts.
GS3.trivia: Microplastics are plastic particles smaller than 5 millimeters, often invisible to the naked eye, found globally.

prelimsSyllabus.topic: Solid Waste Management and Plastic Pollution

GS3.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches Next-Gen Communication Satellite for Enhanced Broadband Services
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched the GSAT-24R communication satellite aboard the GSLV Mk-III rocket from Satish Dhawan Space Centre.
• GSAT-24R is a high-throughput satellite designed to provide advanced broadband connectivity, direct-to-home (DTH) services, and digital data transmission across India and its island territories.
• This mission marks a significant step towards bolstering India's indigenous satellite communication capabilities and reducing reliance on foreign transponders.
• The satellite features state-of-the-art Ku-band transponders, offering increased bandwidth and improved signal quality.
• The successful launch is expected to bridge the digital divide in remote areas and support initiatives like Digital India and BharatNet.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights
prelimsPoints: GSAT-24R: next-gen communication satellite
Launch vehicle: GSLV Mk-III
Purpose: broadband, DTH, digital data services
Features: Ku-band transponders
Launched by ISRO from Satish Dhawan Space Centre
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of indigenous communication satellites like GSAT-24R for India's digital infrastructure and national security.
Analyze how enhanced satellite broadband connectivity can contribute to bridging the digital divide and achieving goals of Digital India.
Evaluate ISRO's role in advancing India's self-reliance in space technology and its commercial potential.
Examine the challenges and opportunities in the global satellite communication market for Indian players.
GS3.imageDescription: A GSLV Mk-III rocket lifting off into space with a trail of fire.
GS3.trivia: ISRO holds the record for launching the most satellites in a single mission (104 satellites in 2017).

GS3.headline: Government Unveils Comprehensive National Cybersecurity Strategy 2.0 to Safeguard Digital Infrastructure
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) launched the National Cybersecurity Strategy 2.0, a significant upgrade to India's cyber defense framework.
• The new strategy emphasizes proactive threat intelligence, critical infrastructure protection, and enhanced public-private partnerships.
• It introduces a dedicated 'National Cyber Force' for rapid response to major cyber incidents and strengthens capacity building in cybersecurity skills.
• Focus areas include securing Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI), developing indigenous cybersecurity products, and fostering international collaboration against cyber threats.
• The strategy aims to create a resilient and secure cyberspace for citizens and businesses, aligning with India's vision for a digital economy.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
Cyber security
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights

Establishment of 'National Cyber Force'
